nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving shallow waters with muddy or sandy bottoms; canals, ponds, and floodplain lakes of the Amazon and Orinoco basins

decorPreferences: robust or plastic plants (real plants will be uprooted)

diet.notes: Primarily accepts sinking cichlid pellets as a staple. Frozen krill, silversides, mysis, and live or frozen earthworms are suitable supplements. Feeder goldfish and other feeder fish are generally advised against due to disease transmission risk and thiaminase content.

goodWith: large South American cichlids of similar size

summary: A large, iconic South American cichlid renowned for its bold personality—Oscars tend to recognize their keepers, beg at the glass, and redecorate their tanks. Best kept singly or as a bonded pair in a spacious, well-filtered aquarium.
careNotes:
Oscars are widely regarded as one of the most interactive freshwater fish in the hobby. They have been reported to recognize individual keepers and can sometimes be trained to accept food from the hand. They tend to rearrange decorations and uproot plants, so robust or artificial plants and securely wedged hardscape are advised. A behavior sometimes called 'playing dead' has been documented — fish may lie on their side motionless, especially when stressed or after introduction to a new tank, though the cause is not fully understood.

Because Oscars are messy, high-waste fish, strong filtration (an oversized canister filter or sump system) is generally advised, along with regular partial water changes. Hole-in-the-head disease — a condition affecting the lateral line and skin around the head, sometimes called HLLE (Head and Lateral Line Erosion) — has been widely reported in Oscars and is thought to be associated with poor water quality, nutritional deficiency, and activated carbon use. A varied diet and consistently clean water are the standard preventive approach.

Oscars are best kept singly or as a confirmed, bonded pair. Two unmatched fish housed together can result in severe aggression. A bonded pair will engage in biparental substrate spawning and guard eggs and fry intensively. Oscars are predators and have been known to eat fish up to half their own body size, so small tankmates should be avoided entirely.

The species has established invasive populations in Florida (USA) and parts of Asia due to aquarium releases. Responsible disposal — never releasing fish into waterways — is essential.

Selectively bred color morphs include Tiger, Red, Albino, Black, and Lemon Oscar variants. Captive specimens typically reach 25–35 cm; wild individuals have been recorded to 45 cm in total length.
breedingNotes: Oscars are biparental substrate spawners, meaning both the male and female share responsibility for guarding eggs and fry. Sexing is difficult because the species is monomorphic — males and females look nearly identical — so pairs generally form through group grow-out, where several juveniles are raised together and allowed to pair naturally. Females have been reported to lay up to 3,000 eggs on a flat substrate, and both parents guard the clutch vigorously. Eggs typically hatch within 2–4 days at recommended temperatures. Breeding in captivity has been reported with some regularity and is generally considered moderate in difficulty; the main challenge lies in identifying and acquiring a compatible pair rather than in the spawning behavior itself.

additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (Cichlidae / Cichliformes), native range (Amazon + Orinoco basins), max size (45.7 cm TL), water parameters (22–25°C, pH 6–8, dH 5–19), and diet (carnivore). SeriouslyFish returned HTTP 520 and was skipped.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Third source cross-check. Confirmed temperature 23–27°C, pH 6–8, gH 5–20, kH 5–20, size 25–30 cm captive, lifespan 8–15 years. Minimum tank listed as 55 gal (208 L) — significantly below the specialist-recommended 400 L; the larger value was retained per playbook tank-size bias rule. Countries of origin list used from this source.

fish.breedingNotes: The Oscar is a biparental substrate spawner, meaning both parents share responsibility for guarding eggs and fry. The main challenge is obtaining a compatible bonded pair, typically done by raising a group of juveniles together and allowing natural pairing to occur. Once a pair is established, spawning tends to occur readily, and both parents guard the clutch with considerable intensity.
